title: On the obligation to remember languages that are no longer profitable
summary: Argue that the survival of a minority language is not the responsibility of the market or the school inspector but a collective ethical obligation — and that 'usefulness' is the wrong axis on which to judge it.
topic: Whether and why we owe something to languages that the market does not sustain
prompt: Write a 600–800-word essay arguing that languages losing speakers carry obligations that economic and educational arguments cannot fully discharge. Avoid the language of cultural-heritage tourism. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'Granted… Even so…' concession. Refer to specific languages (Welsh, Yiddish, Quechua, etc.) where helpful. Treat the question as one of ethics, not branding.
hintStarters: Only rarely does the case for a minority language stand on the ground its defenders themselves prefer…
What the question 'is it useful?' cannot reach is the prior question of…
Granted, the cost of sustaining a language no one needs at work is real. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự truyền dạy giữa các thế hệ
cái chết của một ngôn ngữ
tiếng Wales, Yiddish, Quechua — các ngôn ngữ thiểu số có lịch sử phục hồi khác nhau
quan điểm thị trường hoá về ngôn ngữ

sampleText:
Only rarely does the case for a minority language stand on the ground its defenders themselves prefer. The standard arguments — that bilingualism improves cognitive flexibility, that the language has commercial potential, that translating into and out of it sustains certain professions — are all true, and all beside the central point. They concede in their form what the speakers of the language are usually trying to refuse in their content: that a language must justify itself to a buyer before it is allowed to continue.

This is the utility frame, and it dominates almost every conversation about endangered languages. A government considers whether to fund Welsh-medium education and asks what economic return the investment will produce. A school in the Peruvian Andes considers whether Quechua should be a subject of instruction and asks whether the children's future earnings will be improved. The framing is not malicious. It is the framing within which contemporary public decisions are made. But it carries an assumption — that the value of a language is reducible to what its speakers can do with it in a market — which is, on closer inspection, the assumption a defender of the language ought to refuse.

What the question 'is it useful?' cannot reach is the prior question of what the language is the vehicle of. A language is not a tool that someone happens to own. It is a particular way that a particular group of people has worked out how to perceive the world, name relationships, mark obligations, and tell their dead from their living. To lose that — to allow it to be replaced by a more dominant language because the dominant one is more profitable — is not the same kind of loss as the loss of an inefficient industry. It is the loss of a perception. There is no other holder of it, and no other place from which it can be retrieved.

The utility frame also disguises a moral asymmetry. The choice to speak the dominant language is rarely a free choice; it is made under conditions in which not speaking it carries real penalties — fewer jobs, less schooling, more bureaucracy. The choice to maintain the minority language requires effort, organisation, and the willingness to bear a small daily cost. To describe this as a 'market signal' and conclude that the minority language is naturally declining is to mistake an asymmetric coercion for a preference. Yiddish did not stop being spoken in pre-war Europe because its speakers preferred German. Quechua does not lose ground to Spanish because Quechua-speaking parents have made a careful family calculation. The decline is the product of policy, of stigma, and of a labour market that demands one tongue and tolerates another.

It is the prior obligation, not the contingent usefulness, that defines our responsibility toward inherited languages. Granted, the cost of sustaining a language no one needs at work is real: school hours diverted, public money spent, parents asked to bear an extra cognitive load they did not anticipate. Even so, the comparison the utility frame asks us to make — between the cost of preservation and the convenience of monolingual default — is the wrong comparison. The relevant comparison is between maintaining a language and witnessing the disappearance of a way of being a person that no one else carries. Some costs are not worth refusing, and the moral debts they discharge cannot be settled in any other coin.

None of this requires that every language be saved in its full historical extent, or that monolinguals owe minority-language speakers an apology. The argument is narrower. It is that those of us who happen to have inherited a small language — through grandparents, through a region, through accident of birth — owe it more than we owe the more profitable language we will use to make our living. That obligation is not absolute and cannot be enforced; it is, like most moral obligations, a quiet adjustment of one's life around something other than its convenience. The cost of meeting it is small. The cost of refusing it, distributed across generations, is the slow disappearance of an inheritance that, once gone, cannot be reassembled from the dictionaries.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Only rarely does the case for a minority language stand on the ground its defenders themselves prefer
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Chỉ hiếm khi luận điểm cho một ngôn ngữ thiểu số đứng trên mảnh đất mà chính những người bảo vệ nó ưa thích

sampleAnnotations.phrase: They concede in their form what the speakers of the language are usually trying to refuse in their content
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Chúng nhượng bộ trong hình thức điều mà những người nói ngôn ngữ thường cố từ chối trong nội dung

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the utility frame
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: khung hữu dụng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the framing within which contemporary public decisions are made
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: khung trong đó các quyết định công đương đại được đưa ra

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the value of a language is reducible to what its speakers can do with it in a market

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What the question 'is it useful?' cannot reach is the prior question of what the language is the vehicle of

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Only rarely' to open with measured contrarian framing
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở đầu 'Only rarely does the case for a minority language stand on the ground its defenders themselves prefer' đảo trợ động từ 'does' trước chủ ngữ 'the case' sau cụm phủ định 'Only rarely'. Cấu trúc cho phép bài ngay lập tức nêu lên một quan sát nghịch lý: chính những người bảo vệ ngôn ngữ thiểu số đôi khi tranh luận trên các điều kiện mà họ nên từ chối.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Only rarely does the case for a minority language stand on the ground its defenders themselves prefer' inverts 'does' before the subject after 'Only rarely'. The inversion allows the essay to open with a paradoxical observation about the defenders themselves — performed with formal measure rather than polemic.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to relocate the source of obligation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the prior obligation, not the contingent usefulness, that defines our responsibility toward inherited languages' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'It is X, not Y, that Z' để chuyển nguồn gốc của trách nhiệm từ sự hữu dụng (tuỳ thuộc, có thể mất đi) sang nghĩa vụ tiên quyết (không phụ thuộc vào thị trường). Cleft này thực hiện chính sự đảo ngược lập luận của bài.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the prior obligation, not the contingent usefulness, that defines our responsibility toward inherited languages' uses 'It is X, not Y, that Z' to relocate the source of obligation from market utility to prior moral inheritance. The cleft syntactically performs the essay's reframing of the question.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chỉ hiếm khi luận điểm cho một ngôn ngữ thiểu số đứng trên mảnh đất mà chính những người bảo vệ nó ưa thích. Các lập luận tiêu chuẩn — rằng song ngữ cải thiện tính linh hoạt nhận thức, rằng ngôn ngữ có tiềm năng thương mại, rằng dịch vào và ra khỏi nó duy trì một số nghề nghiệp — đều đúng, và đều bên cạnh điểm trung tâm. Chúng nhượng bộ trong hình thức điều mà những người nói ngôn ngữ thường cố từ chối trong nội dung: rằng một ngôn ngữ phải tự biện minh với một người mua trước khi được phép tiếp tục.

Đây là khung hữu dụng, và nó thống trị hầu như mọi cuộc trò chuyện về các ngôn ngữ nguy cấp. Một chính phủ xem xét có nên tài trợ giáo dục bằng tiếng Wales và hỏi khoản đầu tư sẽ tạo ra lợi nhuận kinh tế gì. Một trường học ở dãy Andes Peru xem xét liệu tiếng Quechua có nên là môn học và hỏi liệu thu nhập tương lai của trẻ em sẽ được cải thiện. Khung này không ác ý. Nhưng nó mang một giả định — rằng giá trị của một ngôn ngữ có thể quy về những gì người nói nó có thể làm với nó trong thị trường — mà, khi xem xét kỹ hơn, là giả định mà người bảo vệ ngôn ngữ nên từ chối.

Những gì câu hỏi 'có hữu ích không?' không thể với tới là câu hỏi trước đó về việc ngôn ngữ là phương tiện của cái gì. Một ngôn ngữ không phải là một công cụ mà ai đó tình cờ sở hữu. Đó là một cách thức cụ thể mà một nhóm người cụ thể đã tìm ra cách nhận thức thế giới, đặt tên các mối quan hệ, đánh dấu nghĩa vụ, và phân biệt người chết khỏi người sống. Mất nó — cho phép nó bị thay thế bởi một ngôn ngữ thống trị hơn vì ngôn ngữ thống trị có lợi nhuận hơn — không cùng loại với sự mất mát của một ngành công nghiệp kém hiệu quả. Đó là sự mất đi một cách nhận thức. Không có người nắm giữ khác, và không nơi nào khác để có thể lấy lại.

Khung hữu dụng cũng nguỵ trang một sự bất đối xứng đạo đức. Lựa chọn nói ngôn ngữ thống trị hiếm khi là lựa chọn tự do; nó được thực hiện trong điều kiện mà không nói nó mang theo những hình phạt thực sự — ít việc làm hơn, ít giáo dục hơn, nhiều thủ tục hành chính hơn. Tiếng Yiddish không ngừng được nói ở châu Âu trước chiến tranh vì người nói nó ưa thích tiếng Đức. Tiếng Quechua không mất đất cho tiếng Tây Ban Nha vì các phụ huynh nói tiếng Quechua đã thực hiện một tính toán gia đình cẩn thận. Sự suy giảm là sản phẩm của chính sách, của sự kỳ thị, và của một thị trường lao động đòi hỏi một ngôn ngữ và dung thứ một ngôn ngữ khác.

Chính nghĩa vụ tiên quyết, không phải sự hữu dụng tuỳ thuộc, mới định nghĩa trách nhiệm của chúng ta đối với các ngôn ngữ kế thừa. Cứ cho là chi phí duy trì một ngôn ngữ không ai cần ở chỗ làm là có thật. Dù vậy, so sánh mà khung hữu dụng yêu cầu chúng ta thực hiện — giữa chi phí bảo tồn và sự tiện lợi của mặc định đơn ngữ — là so sánh sai. So sánh thích hợp là giữa việc duy trì một ngôn ngữ và chứng kiến sự biến mất của một cách thức làm người mà không ai khác mang theo. Một số chi phí không đáng từ chối, và những món nợ đạo đức mà chúng giải toả không thể được thanh toán bằng đồng tiền nào khác.

Không có gì ở đây đòi hỏi rằng mọi ngôn ngữ phải được cứu trong toàn bộ phạm vi lịch sử của nó, hoặc rằng người đơn ngữ nợ những người nói ngôn ngữ thiểu số một lời xin lỗi. Lập luận hẹp hơn. Đó là những ai trong chúng ta tình cờ đã kế thừa một ngôn ngữ nhỏ — qua ông bà, qua một vùng, qua sự ngẫu nhiên của sinh đẻ — nợ nó nhiều hơn chúng ta nợ ngôn ngữ có lợi nhuận hơn mà chúng ta sẽ dùng để kiếm sống. Nghĩa vụ đó không tuyệt đối và không thể được thực thi; nó, giống như hầu hết các nghĩa vụ đạo đức, là một sự điều chỉnh trầm lặng của đời sống xung quanh điều gì đó khác ngoài sự tiện lợi của nó. Chi phí gặp gỡ nó là nhỏ. Chi phí từ chối nó, phân tán qua các thế hệ, là sự biến mất chậm chạp của một di sản, một khi mất đi, không thể được lắp ráp lại từ các cuốn từ điển.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies inversion after 'Only rarely'?
options: Only rarely the case for a minority language stands on the preferred ground.
Only rarely does the case for a minority language stand on the ground its defenders themselves prefer.
The case for a minority language only rarely stands on the preferred ground.
Only rarely is the case for a minority language stand on preferred ground.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Only rarely' the auxiliary 'does' must precede the subject and the lexical verb must be in base form: 'does ... stand', not 'stands'.
mcQuestions.hint: Negative fronting triggers do-support; verb returns to base form.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central objection to the standard arguments for minority languages?
options: They are factually inaccurate
They are too philosophical for ordinary readers
They concede in their form what the speakers of the language are trying to refuse in their content — that a language must justify itself to a buyer
They are too sentimental about heritage
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues the standard arguments — cognitive flexibility, commercial potential — accept the utility frame in form even when they intend to defend the language. The framing itself is the problem.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the opening paragraph's complaint about defenders' arguments.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'the utility frame'?
options: A literal picture frame used in language teaching
A school of philosophy named after a 19th-century thinker
A bureaucratic form for evaluating language programmes
The assumption that the value of a language is reducible to what its speakers can do with it in a market — the framing within which contemporary public decisions are made
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines the utility frame as the dominant assumption about how to value a language — market return and economic outcomes — which it argues is the wrong frame.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the second paragraph for the definition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to relocate the source of our responsibility?
options: It is the prior obligation, not the contingent usefulness, that defines our responsibility toward inherited languages.
Our responsibility comes from the contingent usefulness of languages.
Both prior obligation and usefulness define our responsibility.
Languages need responsibility to define our prior obligation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, that Z' is a cleft that asserts prior obligation as the ground of responsibility and explicitly rejects utility — a C1 structure performing the essay's reframing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is ... not ... that ...' structure.

options: Even so, sustaining a minority language costs nothing. Granted, no comparison is needed.
Granted, the cost of sustaining a language no one needs at work is real. Even so, the comparison the utility frame asks us to make is the wrong comparison.
Granted and even so, both costs and benefits should be tallied.
Even so, the cost is real. Granted, sustaining the language is worth it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted' acknowledges the real cost; 'Even so' refuses the comparison utility demands. Concession then pivot — mandatory order at C1.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about Yiddish in pre-war Europe?
options: Its speakers preferred German for cultural reasons
It was always destined to disappear
It did not stop being spoken because its speakers preferred German — its decline reflects historical violence, not preference
It survives in unchanged form today
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses Yiddish as a counter-example to the 'natural decline' narrative: the loss reflects historical violence, policy, and stigma, not consumer preference.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the paragraph on moral asymmetry.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay describe a minority language?
options: A tool that someone happens to own
An obstacle to economic development
A purely aesthetic object
A particular way that a particular group has worked out how to perceive the world, name relationships, mark obligations, and tell their dead from their living
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay refuses the tool metaphor and substitutes a richer description: language as a particular way of perceiving and organising experience, irreplaceable because no one else carries it.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the third paragraph, after 'a tool that someone happens to own'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'asymmetric coercion' refer to in the essay?
options: The condition in which speakers of a minority language face real penalties (fewer jobs, less schooling, more bureaucracy) for not switching to the dominant language
Government force used in language policy
An asymmetric grammatical structure in certain languages
Coercion exercised by minority language activists
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues 'choice' here is misleading: not speaking the dominant language carries real costs, while maintaining the minority language requires extra effort — the asymmetry is structural coercion, not free preference.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the paragraph about choice and penalties.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's response to the demand that languages prove their usefulness?
options: It accepts the demand and tries to meet it on its own terms
It refuses the demand — because usefulness is a contingent property, while the value of a language is a prior question about what it is the vehicle of
It argues that all languages are equally useful
It claims usefulness can be measured by speakers' incomes
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's central move is to refuse the utility question: the prior question is what the language is the vehicle of — a question utility cannot answer.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the section about what utility cannot reach.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say is the cost of refusing the obligation to a minority language?
options: An economic penalty for the country
Embarrassment in international cultural forums
The slow disappearance of an inheritance that, once gone, cannot be reassembled from the dictionaries
Loss of tourism revenue
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's closing image: the cost is distributed across generations and ends in an irreversible loss — dictionaries can record words but not retrieve perception.

mcQuestions.prompt: Whom does the essay say is bound by the obligation it describes?
options: Every speaker of every language
Only governments and educational institutions
Only academic linguists
Specifically those who happen to have inherited a small language — through grandparents, a region, or accident of birth
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay narrows the obligation to inheritors: 'those of us who happen to have inherited a small language … owe it more than we owe the more profitable language we will use to make our living.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the closing paragraph for the limit of the claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'a quiet adjustment of one's life around something other than its convenience'?
options: A small, ongoing practice — speaking the language at home, in selected settings — that does not require sacrifice of one's main life but does require not optimising purely for convenience
Moving to a country where the minority language is spoken
A formal vow taken in public
An obligation enforced by the state
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay frames the obligation as modest in cost: a quiet adjustment, not an absolute demand. Most moral obligations, the essay argues, take this shape.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at how the essay characterises the obligation's weight.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay characterise the comparison the utility frame asks us to make?
options: As the right and only relevant comparison
As the wrong comparison — between preservation cost and monolingual convenience — when the relevant comparison is between maintenance and the disappearance of a way of being a person
As an unfair comparison rigged against minorities
As a useful starting point that needs refinement
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay rejects the utility comparison and substitutes a different one: not cost-against-convenience but maintenance-against-disappearance of a perception no one else carries.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the Granted/Even so paragraph for the comparison substitution.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say it is NOT arguing?
options: That minority languages deserve attention
That the utility frame is the wrong frame
That every language must be saved in its full historical extent, or that monolinguals owe minority-language speakers an apology
That inheritors of a minority language carry an obligation
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing paragraph explicitly disclaims the maximalist position: 'None of this requires that every language be saved in its full historical extent, or that monolinguals owe minority-language speakers an apology.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's opening.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'moral debt' mean in the essay's vocabulary?
options: A financial obligation owed to a government
A debt that can be repaid with money
A guilt felt by descendants of colonisers
An obligation that arises from inheritance — owed to a language because one has received it, regardless of its market value
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay treats the obligation as a moral debt: one inherits the language, and the inheritance itself creates the duty — it cannot be paid off in any other coin.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the Granted/Even so closing about coin and debt.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a language in your family, region, or background that is in decline? If so, what specifically would be lost if its last speakers stopped passing it on — and is that loss the kind that creates an obligation, or only a regret? If your answer differs from the essay's, where exactly do you depart from it?
